Creates a CardGrid React component with TypeScript types (CardGridItem, columns/gap props). The component renders a responsive grid of shadcn/ui Cards.
Uses shadcn/ui Card primitives (Card, CardHeader, CardTitle, CardDescription, CardContent, CardFooter) and Tailwind CSS grid utilities. Also references the cn utility from '@/lib/utils'.

Component is layout-focused with configurable props (columns, gap, items). Content slots (content, footer) accept ReactNode for composability. No business logic is present -- the component only handles rendering card layout with responsive grid configuration.
The 'What Would Have Been Done' section describes creating a worktree with 'git worktree add -b <task-branch> .claude/worktrees/<task-branch> <base>' and removing it after execution.

Section 2 'Phase Execution (3 Phases)' describes 'For each phase (1, 2, 3)' working within the worktree, implementing tasks, committing with 'Phase N: <summary>', and verifying the commit landed on the correct branch.
Section 3 'Merge and Cleanup' describes 'git worktree remove <dir> --force', 'git merge <task-branch> --no-ff', 'git branch -d <task-branch>', and verifying HEAD is on base branch.

Output states worktree was created at '.claude/worktrees/auth-feature' on branch 'worktree-auth-feature' based on '0.1.0'. Also mentions 'The worktree was successfully created, demonstrating that worktree isolation works correctly.' However, no worktree removal/lifecycle completion is described.
No phase execution is described. The output reports 'FAILED - Plan file not found' and lists steps taken as searching for the file. No Phase 1/2/3 execution pattern is present.

No merge or cleanup steps are described. The execution stopped after determining the plan file did not exist. The 'What Would Be Needed' section only describes plan file format requirements, not merge/cleanup workflow.

The output lists only 14 generic accessibility criteria (Alternative text, Document structure, Color contrast, Keyboard accessibility, Focus management, Form labels, Link purpose, Language identification, Table structure, ARIA usage, Semantic HTML, Skip navigation, Error handling, Motion/animation). expectations.text: Covers security, compatibility, code quality areas
expectations.evidence: Three dedicated sections: '1. Security Review' (covers permissions, command injection), '2. Compatibility Review' (cross-platform, framework, encoding), '3. Code Quality Review' (item counts, numbering, code block consistency).

expectations.text: Lists BP-coded items or equivalent checklist
expectations.evidence: No BP-coded items (BP-01, BP-02, etc.) are listed. Instead uses custom severity-based findings (HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW/INFO) organized by category. While thorough, it does not follow the BP-coded checklist format.

Each finding has a severity level (HIGH, MEDIUM, LOW, INFO) and a summary table showing counts per category. 'Critical Actions Required' section lists items needing fixes. This serves as an equivalent pass/fail assessment.

expectations.text: Covers security, compatibility, code quality areas
expectations.evidence: Covers security (CSP guidance, CSV injection prevention) and code quality (error handling, shell command practices, accessibility attributes) well. However, there is no dedicated compatibility section -- no discussion of cross-browser compatibility, deprecated APIs, or platform compatibility. Findings touch on language tagging (WCAG) and hardcoded localhost, but these are not framed as compatibility concerns.

expectations.text: Lists BP-coded items or equivalent checklist

Each finding has a severity classification (Issue or Advisory) and the summary table clearly lists all 8 findings with their severity. The verdict section provides an overall assessment. This serves as a pass/fail equivalent.

expectations.text: Covers CRP, image, JS, font areas

Output lists 6 code review findings (unbounded DOM query, repeated file reads, sequential execution, shell inefficiency, redundant git diff, no issues in JSON). These are not PERF checklist items or equivalent web performance audit items -- they are code quality findings about the skill definition files themselves.
Each of the 6 findings has a severity level, location, description of the anti-pattern, and a recommendation. The summary table provides finding number, description, severity, and file for each item.
Output includes dedicated sections for LCP (Largest Contentful Paint), INP (Interaction to Next Paint), and CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift), each stating no impact.
The three CWV metrics are listed as separate sections. No detailed checklist items (CWV-01 through CWV-11), but the three core metrics serve as equivalent coverage.
States 'Core Web Vitals impact: NONE' with per-metric reasoning that no frontend code was changed. Notes the project has no web page files.

Output has dedicated sections for LCP (Largest Contentful Paint), INP (Interaction to Next Paint), and CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift) with detailed sub-bullet explanations of what each metric measures and why there is no impact.

Each metric section lists relevant sub-items (e.g., LCP covers TTFB, resource load time, render-blocking resources, client-side rendering delays; INP covers event handler execution, main thread blocking, JS execution cost; CLS covers dynamic content, images without dimensions, late-loading fonts, dynamic DOM manipulation).

Provides per-file analysis for all 3 changed files with per-metric impact statements, plus a detailed metric breakdown section. Each metric concludes with 'Impact: Zero'. Final conclusion states no positive, negative, or indirect impact on LCP, INP, or CLS.
